coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical biosensor based on PAMAM functionalized MXene nanoplatform for detection of folate receptor
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Jiayi
Luo, Linghuan
Ying, Na
Wu, Shu
Ji, Jun
Su, Haoyuan
Li, Xiaoou
Zeng, Dongdong
coredata.dc:description: The level of folate receptor (FR) has become one of the independent factors for measuring human tumor diseases. The precise quantification of FR is helpful for the early diagnosis and subsequent treatment of tumors. The modification of electrodes is a key issue in ensuring and enhancing the electrochemical biosensing ability. In this study, we in-situ synthesized a nanocomposite material with excellent conductivity and stability by grafting first-generation poly(amidoamine) dendrimers onto the MXene (Ti3C2TX) as the immobilized matrix (PAMAM@MXene). An electrochemical sensor was developed for FR monitor by loading the PAMAM@MXene on screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) supported the effective synthesis of PAMAM@MXene. Under optimal conditions, the prepared sensor achieved the quantification of FR with a wide range of concentrations from 10 ng/mL to 1000 ng/mL with a detection limit (LOD) of 5.6 ng/mL. It also exhibited satisfactory selectivity, reproducibility, and stability, which provided the possibility for expanding new pathways in the detection of clinical FR.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Folate receptor
MXene
In-situ synthesis
Electrochemical biosensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical response of FR on diverse modified electrodes amperometry
Selectivity, anti-interference ability, stability, and reproducibility of the sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shanghai University of Medicine and Health Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Academic Mentorship for Scientific Research Cadre Project of Shanghai University of Medicine and Health Sciences ( 2021-4 ) and Shanghai Municipal Health Commission Research project plan (202340295).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of MXene (A) and PAMAM@MXene (B). (C) Elemental mapping images of the PAMAM@MXene composite.

Water contact angles for the bare SPCE (A), MXene/SPCE (B) and PAMAM@MXene/SPCE (C). CV curves of bare SPCE (D), MXene/SPCE (E) and PAMAM@MXene/SPCE (F). EIS changes of bare SPCE (G), MXene/SPCE (H) and PAMAM@MXene/SPCE (I). Inset showed the Randles equivalent circuit, where Rs, CDL and W denote solution resistance, double-layer capacitance and Warburg impedance, respectively.

CV curve (A) and the corresponding peak current graph (B) of bare SPCE without FR (curve a) and with FR (curve b, 100 ng/mL). CV curve (C) and the corresponding peak current graph (D) of MXene/SPCE without FR (curve a) and with FR (curve b, 100 ng/mL). CV curve (E) and the corresponding peak current graph (F) of PAMAM@MXene/SPCE without FR (curve a) and with FR (curve b, 100 ng/mL). The corresponding current values of bare SPCE, MXene/SPCE and PAMAM@MXene/SPCE with FR were 0.96, 0.88, and 0.75-fold of that without FR, respectively. (G) Comparison of current response of different electrodes without FR (curve a) and with FR (curve b, 100 ng/mL). Δ I = Base current (curve a) − Anodic peak current (curve b). Error bars represented standard deviation (n = 3). All the above experiments were performed in 100 mM KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at a scanning sate of 100 mV/s.

Effects of film thickness of PAMAM@MXene (A) and the incubation time of FR (B), the CV responses obtained from PAMAM@MXene/SPCE in 100 mM KCl containing 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3−/4−, at 100 mV s−1. Error bars represented standard deviation (n = 3).
(A) DPVs of PAMAM@MXene/SPCE with diverse content of FR: 10, 100, 400, 800 and 1000 ng/mL. (B) Calibration curve of peak current vs FR content. Error bars represented standard deviation (n = 3).

(A) Selectivity of the PAMAM@MXene/SPCE with the addition of 100-fold concentration of interference molecules by CV techniques. Error bars represented standard deviation (n = 3). Statistical analysis: ***, p < 0.001. (B) The peak current values of FR at PAMAM@MXene/SPCE in the presence of different interferents (Mix: FR + thrombin + BSA + lgG). Error bars represented standard deviation (n = 3). Statistical analysis: ns, not significant. (C) 100 segments continuous CV scanning of PAMAM@MXene/SPCE at 100 mV s−1. (D) Stability measurements of the prepared sensor against 100 ng/mL FR for 7 days. Error bars represented standard deviation (n = 3).
Schematic diagram of the PAMAM@MXene synthesizing procedure and the sensing strategy for detecting FR.
Comparison of various FR detection technology.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical biosensor based on PAMAM functionalized MXene nanoplatform for detection of folate receptor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai University of Medicine & Health Sciences, Shanghai 201318, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai University of Medicine & Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai University of Medicine & Health Sciences, Shanghai 201318, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The level of folate receptor (FR) has become one of the independent factors for measuring human tumor diseases. The precise quantification of FR is helpful for the early diagnosis and subsequent treatment of tumors. The modification of electrodes is a key issue in ensuring and enhancing the electrochemical biosensing ability. In this study, we in-situ synthesized a nanocomposite material with excellent conductivity and stability by grafting first-generation poly(amidoamine) dendrimers onto the MXene (Ti3C2TX) as the immobilized matrix (PAMAM@MXene). An electrochemical sensor was developed for FR monitor by loading the PAMAM@MXene on screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) supported the effective synthesis of PAMAM@MXene. Under optimal conditions, the prepared sensor achieved the quantification of FR with a wide range of concentrations from 10 ng/mL to 1000 ng/mL with a detection limit (LOD) of 5.6 ng/mL. It also exhibited satisfactory selectivity, reproducibility, and stability, which provided the possibility for expanding new pathways in the detection of clinical FR.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Folate receptor (FR) is a glycosyl phosphatidylinositol-anchored membrane glycoprotein, which is overexpressed in many human tumors, including ovarian cancer, lung cancer, breast cancer, and kidney cancer [1–3]. In contrast, it is expressed at low levels in healthy cells. According to the report, FR can serve as an independent risk factor and provide key information for disease progression and clinical evaluation [4]. Therefore, establishing an accurate and rapid method for FR detection is crucial for the early diagnosis and subsequent treatment of related malignant tumors.

Currently, varieties of many analytical techniques were applied to detect FR, such as fluorescence [5–8], colorimetric assay [9,10], positron emission tomography imaging (PET imaging) [11,12], and electrochemistry [13,14]. Among them, electrochemical technology, as an emerging analytical tool with characteristics such as fast response speed, high sensitivity, good portability, low cost, and easy integration, has been applied in the medical field successfully [15–17]. However, traditional electrodes are no longer able to cope with more refined and complex detection needs. Accordingly, there remain challenges in constructing an efficient, sensitive and stable electrochemical sensor for quantitative analysis of FR.

The electrode-modified nanomaterial is a key factor in constructing high-performance electrochemical biosensing interfaces. MXene, a layered two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterial similar to graphene, is a collective name for emerging transition metal carbides and nitrides [18–20]. Because of its excellent electrical properties, high conductivity, good biocompatibility, excellent mechanical and energy storage performance, MXene has broad application value in fields such as supercapacitors [21,22], catalysts [23], and lithium (Li) -ion batteries [24,25]. Especially, as MXene has a large surface area and rich functional groups, it has satisfied the ideal characteristics of electrochemical promising materials [26]. Recently, sensors based-MXene have been widely used to detect biomolecules such as glucose [27,28], nucleic acids [29], uric acid (UA) [30], cholesterol cholesterol [31], etc., and have shown satisfactory sensing capabilities. For example, Li et al. hybridized platinum (Pt) on MXene to structure electrochemical sensors for glucose detection under neutral conditions [32]. Shankar et al. fabricated MXene/graphite composite paste electrode for electrochemical detecting l-cysteine [26]. Yang et al. successfully prepared ratiometric electrochemical biosensor for the accurate detection of carcinoembryonic antigen and demonstrated excellent antifouling performance [33].

Although people have found that MXene has the disadvantage of aggregation in long-term research, which limits the catalytic activity of the platform. However, it has been proven that the incorporation of superior nanomaterials into the structure of MXene can further improve electrochemical sensing performance [27]. Poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM) dendrimers belong to a new type of nanomaterials with controllable size, excellent chemical stability, rich functional groups, three-dimensional and hyper-branched structures [34–37]. Their surfaces easily to be functionalized as well as numerous interaction sites provide promising prerequisites for the binding of other nanomaterials and immobilization of the biorecognition molecules [38–43]. These outstanding properties have brought them much attention in the field of biosensors [44]. For example, Li and co-researcher modified gold bead electrode with Prussian blue (PB) investigating nano-Au/PAMAM dendrimer to afford electrocatalytic layers, which achieved the stability and high sensitivity detection of hydrogen peroxide [45]. Sebokolodi et al. developed an electrochemical sensor based on CNF-PAMAM modified glassy carbon electrode. After measurement, the modified electrode showed improved detection efficiency and electrochemical response to nicotine [46].

In this work, composites of PAMAM@MXene were constructed and modified on the screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) as the sensing interface for qualitative detection of FR. Scheme 1 showed the detection process and sensing substrate of the electrochemical platform. PAMAM@MXene was fabricated through the in-situ synthesis of PAMAM on MXene (Ti3C2TX) nanosheets and fixed on the bare electrode surface through drop-casting. This composite system integrated the ultra-high conductivity of MXene and the chemical stability of PAMAM, and greatly improved the adsorption capacity of biomolecules. The biorecognition mechanism of this experiment was based on the specific interaction between Folic acid (FA) and FR. When the target existed, a large amount of protein antigen was captured onto the modified electrode to form an insulating interface layer that hindered electron transfer, and the peak response of DPV current decreased. Compared with traditional detection methods, this biosensor had the characteristics of high sensitivity, strong specificity, and low cost, which was expected to provide a more efficient and stable detection configuration for clinical diagnosis of FR.

All chemicals were used directly in this study. FA and 1-ethyl - (3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), 1 × PBS buffer and FR were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Succinic anhydride, anhydrous tetrahydrofuran, thionyl chloride (Socl2), Ti3C2TX MXene, thrombin, immunoglobulin G (IgG), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and other chemicals were obtained from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water for the preparation of all solutions.

Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out on CHI660B (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, China) with a three-electrode system, which contained carbon, Ag/AgCl and platinum (Pt) as working, reference and counter electrode respectively. Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Regulus 8100, Guoyi Quantum Technology Co., Ltd., China) was used for microstructure characterization and material composition analysis. All electrochemical measurements were conducted in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- used as an electrolyte in the potential range of −0.2 to 0.6 V.

Dispersing Ti3C2Tx MXene (0.25 g) and succinic anhydride (2.5 g) in 25 mL ethanol solution using magnetic stirring at room temperature for 24 h. The product was washed with ultrapure water and centrifuged several times, then dried under vacuum to obtain the MXene-COOH. Details on the synthesis of PAMAM@MXene are listed in ESI.

The screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) was polished using Plasma Cleaner for 3 mins to get a clean and easily coated surface. Next, PAMAM@MXene powder was dissolved in ultrapure water (5 mg/mL) by bath sonication under N2 for 20 mins (25 °C). After centrifugation, the ultimate obtained supernatant was stored at 4 °C for further use. Finally, 4 μL suspension was dropped on the surface of SPCE and dried at a natural environment for solvent evaporation to form films. Similarly, SPCE decorated with MXene was prepared by the same procedure.
FA was coupled to PAMAM@MXene through carbodiimide reaction in this experiment. In short, add FA (1 mg) and EDC (8.65 mg) to DMF and DMSO solutions (2 mL, 3:1, v/v) and stir for 3 h.

Subsequently, the activated FA was immobilized onto the PAMAM@MXene/SPCE surface by drop casting method to obtain FA/PAMAM@MXene/SPCE. Next, the fabricated FA/PAMAM@MXene/SPCE was rinsed with PBS to remove any excess materials. Finally, the biosensing platform was incubated in 4 µL of FR with different concentrations at 37 °C for 40 mins.

The surface morphology of the nanocomposites was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, MXene presented a typical 2D layered structure, with a smooth and compact stack of thin slices. After the introduction of PAMAM dendrimer into MXene, the structural morphology of MXene remained flat and lamellar (Fig. 1B), but the lamellar stack was dispersed and the gap surface roughness increased significantly, which was conducive to providing a larger active surface area. Fig. S1 was an Energy-Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDS) of the composite material, which verified the existence of elements such as C, N, O, and Ti.

Subsequently, the chemical state of the surface powder element was analyzed by elemental mapping images (Fig. 1C). We can observe that C, O, N, and Ti elements were distributed homogeneously, which reveal the successful modification of PAMAM on MXene. In the composite materials studied in this study, PAMAM grow uniformly on the layered surface of MXene to prevent the oxidation and agglomeration, while MXene acted as a core matrix for the synthesis of PAMAM.

In order to determine the affinity and spreading ability of the obtained nanomaterials, we conducted the static water contact angle experiments. As shown in Fig. 2 A and B, the CA for the bare SPCE surface was 121.6° ± 1.1° and decreased to 75.5° ± 1.4° after the modification with MXene on the carbon surface. In addition, PAMAM functionalized MXene showed improved hydrophilicity, with the CA reduced to 52.6° ± 1.9° (Fig. 2C). This may be ascribed to the presence of a large number of functional groups such as hydroxyl and amino groups in MXene and PAMAM. The decrease in CA indicated that PAMAM functionalized MXene nanolayers can provide a biocompatible surface because of their inherent properties that may enhance the loading capacity of biomolecules.

The electrochemical behaviors of electrodes modified with different materials have been investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), and the results of which were as shown in Fig. 2D ∼ F. The anode peak current for the bare SPCE and MXene/SPCE were determined as 59.7 ± 0.4 μA and 70.2 ± 0.4 μA respectively. The results clearly showed that casting MXene onto the bare SPCE surface helped to increase the peak current, as MXene has a two-dimensional layered structure with excellent conductivity and specific surface area. As anticipated, the peak current of PAMAM@MXene/SPCE had gone up to 82.5 ± 2.2 μA following the integration of prepared mixed materials on the SPCE surface, indicating that the in-situ synthesis of PAMAM increased the surface area of MXene. Furthermore, the cathodic peak currents of the bare SPCE, MXene/SPCE and PAMAM@MXene/SPCE were 57.3 ± 0.8 μA, 72.5 ± 3.5 μA, and 81.0 ± 1.8 μA, respectively. Compared with the previous two electrodes, the electrode modified with PAMAM@MXene represent the best current response, indicating that PAMAM@MXene nanocomposites accelerated electron transfer.

EIS, consisting of the semicircle part and linear part, was carried to characterize the interfacial properties of different modified electrodes. Specifically, the blocking process of electron transfer and the diffusion of redox molecules were represented by the high frequency semicircular part with a diameter equivalent to the electron transfer resistance (Rct) and the low frequency linear part respectively [47]. As displayed in Fig. 2G and H, the bare SPCE showed Rct value of 8304 Ω even after a thorough cleaning process, while a significant decrease in Rct was observed on MXene/SPCE (Rct = 4681 Ω). Owing to large effective surface areas and electrical conductivity, MXene promotes charge-transfer rate between the electrode and [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- electroactive pairs. It was worth noting that, the Rct was further decreased to 648 Ω after the successive modification on the surface of SPCE by PAMAM@MXene, indicating that the nanocomposite material effectively improved electrochemical activity and had higher conductivity (Fig. 2I). This result was consistent with the above conclusion. To summarize, PAMAM@MXene/SPCE showed outstanding electrocatalytic ability, excellent conductivity and potential practicality of biological analysis of the nano sensing platform.

While in the low frequency region, the different modified electrodes showed similar characteristics. A slope of itscorresponding linear part was presented as 45°, which indicated that the capacitive process was controlled by representative Warburg diffusion [48]. It is noteworthy that the solution resistance (Rs) was about 100 Ω in the ultrahigh frequency region.

The kinetic of the PAMAM@MXene coated electrode reaction was under further investigation by CV at a scan rate (v) of 30 mV/s to 120 mV/s. As shown in Fig. S2A and B, the intensity of anodic and cathodic peak currents (Ipa and Ipc) of the modified electrode varied linearly with increasing square root of the scan rate. The corresponding fitting equation were Ipa(µA) = 8.275v1/2(mV/s)1/2 + 6.424 (R2 = 0.9987) and Ipc(µA) = −6.544v1/2(mV/s)1/2-16.92 (R2 = 0.9986), respectively. This result demonstrated that the electrochemical catalytic reaction on the electrode surface was a diffusion-controlled process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical response of FR on diverse modified electrodes amperometry
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the electrochemical performance of diverse modified electrodes, we evaluated and compared the current response of bare SPCE, MXene/SPCE and PAMAM@MXene/SPCE in the absence and presence of 100 ng/mL FR (Fig. 3 ). As illustrated in Fig. 3A, the current response of curve b decreased slightly compared to curve a, indicating that FR can be successfully detected by bare SPCE. But the corresponding data showed that the current after adding FR was 0.96-fold that of the SPCE without FR, indicating poor electrochemical performance of the bare electrode (Fig. 3B). In contrast, the peak current of MXene/SPCE decreased evidently after adding FR, with a current value 0.88-fold that of the base current, indicating that MXene can improve the conductivity of the bare electrode (Fig. 3C and D). Interestingly, PAMAM@MXene/SPCE had a more significant current difference after adding FR, from 82.5 ± 2.2 μA decreased to 61.6 ± 2.3 μA (Fig. 3E and F). Besides, it also exhibited a more negative anodic peak potential (+0.250 V) compared to the bare SPCE (+0.310 V). Calculated, PAMAM@MXene/SPCE had the highest electrochemical catalysis. The current difference of PAMAM@MXene/SPCE and MXene/SPCE were 8.0 and 3.1 times that of bare SPCE, respectively (Fig. 3G). These characteristics were mainly attributed to the following factors. First, the excellent conductivity of MXene can accelerate electron transport on the bare SPCE surface. Secondly, PAMAM macromolecules can improve the oxidative stacking phenomenon of MXene due to their excellent stability, providing more active sites. In addition, PAMAM contains abundant amino functional groups, which allows it to adsorb more biological molecules to the electrode surface for sensing detection. Therefore, compared to all the other electrodes, PAMAM@MXene/SPCE had a higher electrochemical response and abundant active sites for loading FR.

We optimized the film thickness of the PAMAM@MXene used to modify the electrode by controlling different droplet volume. As shown in Fig. S5, the film thickness raised by increasing the droplet volume, and the calibration curve with a linear regression equation was Y = 9.574X + 38.24 (R2 = 0.9945) (Where X was the droplet volume in μL and Y was the film thickness in nm). Fig. 4 A optimized the film thickness of final synthetic material used to modify the bare electrode. We can see that as the film thickness increased from 55.4 nm to 78.0 nm, the current response also increased and reached a maximum at a film thickness of 78.0 nm. This is because an appropriate film thickness can increase the active surface area of the working electrode, providing more catalytic sites. However, PAMAM@MXene film developed was excessively thick, it would produce a surface stacking effect, which hindered electron transfer and reduced the electrochemical response. So, in this experiment, 78.0 nm was selected as the optimal film thickness.

Incubation time have an impact on the performance of electrochemical biosensors for FR detection. We investigated the hybridization between FA and FR with the incubation time from 20 to 100 mins. As can be seen in Fig. 4B, with the increase of time, the response current value continuously decreased until achieving minimum at 40 mins. This might be the fact that the folate receptor was captured to the electrode surface in large quantities by antigen–antibody specific binding. The immune complexes formed blocked the transfer of electrons, which leaded to a drop in current. And as time increased, the antibody antigen gradually reacted completely. Subsequently, the peak current increased and tended to flatten over time beyond 40 mins, which may be due to reduced activity of partial antibodies as a result of prolonged incubation. Therefore, 40 mins was chosen as the ideal incubation time for this experiment.

In this study, we investigated DPV responses of the prepared sensor to FR of different target concentrations (10, 100, 400, 800, 1000 ng/mL). When FR was specifically recognized and captured onto the modified SPCE, an obstacle layer for electron transfer is formed, resulting in a decrease in the peak current. As shown in Fig. 5A, a noticeable decrease in the peak current of FR was discovered with the increase of its concentrations. In addition, from Fig. 5 B, it can be seen that the peak current was proportional to the concentrations of FR, and the linear fitting equation is Y = -0.02676X + 74.18 (R2 = 0.9916) (Where X was the FR concentration in ng/mL and Y was the electrochemical signal value in µA). The detection limit (LOD) was calculated by the formula LOD = 3S/N, where S was the standard deviation of blank samples and N was the slope of the calibration curve. After calculation, the LOD of FR was determined to be 5.6 ng/mL.

The different detection techniques for FR were showed in Table 1 . Compared with previously reported methods, the proposed sensor was cost-effective and easy to operate. Secondly, the multi-channel screen-printed carbon electrode used in this experiment had advantages of rapid and efficient detection. Finally, the sensor had a wide linear range while maintaining a relatively low detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, anti-interference ability, stability, and reproducibility of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the accuracy of detecting FR in clinical samples, this experiment measured the following indicator factors, including selectivity, anti-interference, stability, and reproducibility. The interfering substances such as lgG, BSA, and thrombin were selected for testing selectivity and the anti-interference ability of the probe. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the response signals of lgG, BSA, and thrombin (10 µg/mL) were similar to the blank signal value. However, only when FR (100 ng/mL) was present can the variation of current signals be detected. Further, after adding 100 times the interferences, there has litter change in the peak current of detecting FR (Fig. 6B). The above results showed that the sensor had excellent selectivity and preeminent anti-interference performance. Apart from that, scanning 100 segments on the modified electrode, the response current value was basically unaffected (Fig. 6C). Meanwhile, after being stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for one week, the peak current of the embellished electrode for detecting FR (100 ng/mL) only decreased by 1.6 % compared to the initial current (Fig. 6D), which confirmed its gratifying stability. Finally, five nanocomposite modified electrodes were prepared under the same conditions to detect FR (50 ng/mL) respectively. After calculation, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the peak current was less than 1.7 %. In summary, the sensor exhibited outstanding selectivity, anti-interference ability, stability, and reproducibility, making it applicable for complex biological samples.

In conclusion, we have prepared PAMAM@MXene composite materials were first used for the detection of FR. In the light of SEM and EDS analyses, PAMAM was successfully synthesized on the MXene layered surface. It was worth mentioning that the synthesis of PAMAM effectively prevents the stacking effect of MXene, further enhancing the stability and electrochemical characteristics of modified electrodes. The research showed that this sensor has a wide detection range and low detection limit for FR specific detection. In addition, the platform also showed wonderful anti-interference, repeatability, and specificity. This experiment has potential value in clinical judgment of FR-related tumor diseases.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Academic Mentorship for Scientific Research Cadre Project of Shanghai University of Medicine and Health Sciences (2021-4) and Shanghai Municipal Health Commission Research project plan (202340295).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioelechem.2023.108627.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
